title: The Ceasefire That Keeps Breaking
subhead: A week that began with cautious optimism from Switzerland ended with US airstrikes on Iranian soil, Iranian missiles on US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain, and a ceasefire in genuine danger of collapse. Three separate armistices — Iran, Lebanon, Gaza — proved the same rule: agreements on paper don't stop shooting on the ground. Elsewhere, Europe's worst heatwave on record killed hundreds, and Venezuela's earthquake toll passed 1,400.

sections.heading: US-Iran Ceasefire on the Brink After Two Escalation Cycles
sections.body:
The 14-point memorandum of understanding signed on June 17 survived its first week — barely. On Thursday, Iran fired a drone that struck the Singapore-flagged cargo ship Ever Lovely in the Strait of Hormuz, damaging its bridge. The US responded with strikes on Iranian missile and drone storage facilities on Qeshm Island. Iran's parliamentary security chief Ebrahim Azizi called the drone attack 'ceasefire management,' adding 'the Strait of Hormuz is governed by Iran, so: Respect the rules.'

Three days later, it happened again. Iran launched a one-way attack drone at the Panama-flagged tanker MT Kiku — carrying crude for Qatar, one of the deal's mediators. The US struck Iran a second time, hitting surveillance infrastructure, air defense sites, drone storage, and minelayer capabilities across multiple locations. Iran immediately retaliated, firing ballistic missiles and drones at US military bases in Kuwait and Bahrain, targeting Ali al-Salem base and the Fifth Naval Fleet at Port Salman. Kuwait activated air defenses and told citizens to seek shelter.

Trump wrote that 'it may come a point when we are no longer able to be reasonable' and warned that the Islamic Republic 'will no longer exist.' Iran's foreign ministry called the US a 'treaty-breaking regime.' Neither side has formally withdrawn from the MOU, but the de-escalation mechanism is failing: each side sees its own actions as enforcement and the other's as violation. The 60-day negotiating window is now nine days in and the most basic premise — that both sides will refrain from using force — is being tested with live munitions.
sections.whyItMatters: Two escalation cycles in three days is not a ceasefire holding under strain; it is a ceasefire unraveling. The fundamental dispute over who controls Hormuz is being settled by force on the water while diplomats negotiate in Switzerland. If the next incident produces American or Iranian casualties, the MOU may not survive.

sections.heading: Switzerland Talks Open Against a Backdrop of Hardline Resistance
sections.body:
US Vice President JD Vance and Iranian negotiators Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf and Abbas Araghchi met in Obbuergen, Switzerland, for the first round of technical talks following the MOU. Mediators Pakistan and Qatar announced a 60-day roadmap toward a final deal, with working groups on nuclear, economic, and regional security tracks. Vance said the talks laid 'a very good foundation,' acknowledging 'the final deal is the house — we set the foundation, we haven't built the house.'

But the talks were rocky from the start. Trump threatened on social media that the US would strike Iran 'harder' if Iran does not stop Hezbollah, prompting the Iranian delegation to temporarily withdraw. Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson Esmail Baghaei said negotiations toward a final agreement would only begin once 'key commitments are upheld,' warning that otherwise 'the memorandum of understanding as a whole will be jeopardized.' Senior Iranian parliament member Ebrahim Rezaei dismissed the 14-point MOU as a 'wish list.' Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei issued a rare written statement saying he 'held a different view' but approved the deal after President Pezeshkian accepted responsibility. Hardline cleric Mahmoud Nabavian went on state television reading what he claimed was correspondence between the supreme leader and the security council, asserting Khamenei opposed the ceasefire.

The sequencing dispute is fundamental: Iran insists that Lebanon ceasefire clauses and economic relief must be implemented before nuclear negotiations begin. The US wants nuclear inspections first. The IAEA's Rafael Grossi said inspections 'will indeed take place' under the MOU; Iran's deputy foreign minister responded that access to bombed sites would 'only be addressed within the framework of a final agreement.' Before the war, the IAEA reported Iran had 440 kg of uranium enriched to 60% — near weapons grade, enough for up to 10 bombs. Without access to the sites the US bombed, the IAEA cannot verify where that material is now.
sections.whyItMatters: The 60-day clock is running on a negotiation that the 2015 JCPOA took two years to complete. Iran's hardliners are openly hostile, the supreme leader disavowed the deal, and the two sides fundamentally disagree on the order of implementation. The Switzerland talks are the mechanism for turning the MOU into a permanent agreement — but the mechanism is already creaking.

sections.heading: Hormuz: 500 Ships Trapped, Evacuation Frozen, Two Routing Systems Competing
sections.body:
Before the war, more than 130 vessels transited the Strait of Hormuz daily. The MOU was supposed to restore that flow. Instead, the strait has become a live conflict zone. After the Ever Lovely drone strike on Thursday, the International Maritime Organization paused its evacuation of more than 11,000 stranded sailors. About 115 vessels managed to leave before the operation was halted; approximately 500 ships remain trapped. After Iran's IRGC warned that vessels using unauthorized routes would not be guaranteed safe passage, at least two tankers reversed course, according to Lloyd's List Intelligence.

Two competing routing systems are now operating. The US Navy's Joint Maritime Information Center expanded an alternative corridor hugging Oman's coast, allowing both inbound and outbound traffic — directly challenging Iran's assertion that it controls transit. Iran's newly created Persian Gulf Strait Authority insists vessels must use Tehran-designated routes or forfeit safe-passage guarantees and has not ruled out charging tolls after the 60-day window. Marine data firm Windward called it 'the first significant test' of the deal's normalization: 'The pace of normalization has slowed.'

The economic stakes are large. The US Treasury lifted sanctions on Iranian oil exports for 60 days, authorizing purchases in US dollars. Brent crude fell below $72.48 — the pre-war level — before edging back to $73.23 after the first drone strike. Iran confirmed the release of $12 billion in frozen assets, with central bank governor Hemmati working on releasing $6 billion more from Qatar. But many buyers remain cautious given the 60-day horizon and snapback risk. Trump separately suggested the US could impose its own tolls for 'services rendered as the Guardian Angel to the countries of the Middle East' — a proposal legal experts say would violate the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea.
sections.whyItMatters: The strait is where the deal meets reality. With 500 ships still trapped, the IMO evacuation paused, and two incompatible routing frameworks in operation, Hormuz is a microcosm of the ceasefire's core contradiction: both sides claim to want free passage, but both are using force to enforce their own version of it.

sections.heading: Israel-Lebanon Framework Signed, Rejected by Hezbollah, Undermined by Violence
sections.body:
Israel and Lebanon signed a US-brokered four-point framework agreement in Washington on Friday, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio calling it a step toward 'lasting peace and security.' Lebanon's ambassador to the US, Nada Hamadeh, described it as enabling 'our people to go back to their land.' The agreement affirms each state's right to live in peace, commits to the release of detainees, and envisions the Lebanese Armed Forces taking exclusive control of territory after 'verified disarmament of non-state armed groups.'

But the agreement was undercut from every direction. Hezbollah rejected it outright — leader Naim Qassem called it 'humiliating, shameful, and a surrender of sovereignty,' declared it 'null and void,' and specifically criticized linking Israel's withdrawal to Hezbollah's disarmament as crossing 'all red lines.' Netanyahu then called the deal 'a great achievement for Israel' while stating Israel will remain in the security zone in southern Lebanon 'until Hezbollah is disarmed and no longer poses a threat.' Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz ordered troops to 'prepare for an extended stay' in a zone up to 10km inside Lebanese territory. Israeli strikes killed at least two people in southern Lebanon on Tuesday and another person in Nabatieh al-Fawqa on Friday — hours after the signing.

Israel now controls roughly 60% of Gaza and occupies about 5% of Lebanon. Iran conditioned reopening Hormuz on Israel ending its Lebanon campaign. The framework may be designed partly to unlock that pressure point, but with Hezbollah rejecting it and Israel digging in, it risks becoming a diplomatic veneer over an unresolved military confrontation. At least 4,192 people have been killed in Israeli attacks in Lebanon since March.
sections.whyItMatters: The first formal agreement between Israel and Lebanon is significant as diplomacy — but Hezbollah was not at the table and rejects its terms, Israel continues to expand its occupation, and the killings haven't stopped. The framework links withdrawal to disarmament, a condition that has stalled every previous Lebanon negotiation.

sections.heading: Congress Revolts on Iran War as Trump Requests $87.6 Billion
sections.body:
The White House formally asked Congress to approve $87.6 billion, with $67 billion earmarked for the Pentagon under 'Operation Epic Fury' — including $21 billion for munitions, $17.3 billion for operational costs, and $12.1 billion for classified programs. The request bundles $11 billion for farmers and $1.4 billion for an Ebola response in Central Africa, making it harder to oppose outright but raising questions about packaging domestic spending with war funding.

The request landed amid significant congressional pushback. The Republican-controlled Senate passed a war powers resolution 50-48 directing the president to end US military involvement in Iran unless Congress authorises it. Five Republican senators broke ranks. Senator Bill Cassidy of Louisiana confronted Trump at a closed-door lunch, telling him 'you have not told the American people what's going on' and that the war was 'supposed to last four weeks, it's lasted four months.' Cassidy later reversed course after briefings from Vance and Witkoff, voting to defeat a second war powers measure. Trump branded the Republican dissenters as 'losers.'

The Pentagon's chief financial officer has put the war's cost so far at $29 billion, though defense analysts and lawmakers say that understates the full toll. The $87.6 billion request — coming alongside Trump's broader $1.5 trillion defence budget — will force a public accounting the White House has avoided. Democratic Senator Patty Murray told Hegseth Congress was spending 'families' hard-earned tax dollars on a war that many strongly oppose.'
sections.whyItMatters: Trump can probably get the money, but the political cost is mounting. A four-month war with no clear end state is eroding congressional tolerance, and the $87 billion ask will force a debate about the war's trajectory that the administration has so far avoided. If the 60-day negotiating window stalls, this vote will be the battlefield.

sections.heading: Europe's Record Heatwave: Three National All-Time Records in Two Days
sections.body:
An unprecedented early-summer heatwave shattered all-time temperature records across Europe this week. Germany recorded 41.5°C in Mockern-Drewitz on Saturday, breaking a record set just a day earlier. Denmark hit 37°C, exceeding its previous all-time record from 1976. The Czech Republic recorded 40.8°C in Doksany. Switzerland broke its hottest-June-day record for the third consecutive day. France recorded its hottest day since 1947, with temperatures reaching 43°C in Poitou-Charentes. The UK saw its hottest June day on record at 36.1°C. An estimated 150 million people across Europe experienced temperatures above 35°C.

The human toll mounted rapidly. Spain's MoMo monitoring system recorded 327 heat-attributable deaths between Sunday and Thursday. France reported at least 55 drowning deaths as people sought relief in unsupervised waters. A three-year-old child was found dead in a car near Paris. Paris banned public alcohol consumption during peak heat hours after the city's ambulance service reported four times the normal rate of cardiac arrests. The head of the Rennes emergency department linked five or six home deaths to the heat, noting 'it's not just the very elderly; it's people aged 60 and up.'

Infrastructure buckled. Three French nuclear plants went offline because river cooling water exceeded 28°C. The Golfech plant was shut down entirely. The Louvre and the Eiffel Tower closed early; the Louvre's spokesperson said the building was 'not sufficiently adapted to climate change.' Florence's Uffizi halted ticket sales because its air conditioning couldn't cope. Scientists from World Weather Attribution said a heatwave of this magnitude so early in summer would have been 'virtually impossible' 50 years ago and that climate change is 'unequivocally' to blame. Europe is the fastest-warming continent, heating at twice the global average.
sections.whyItMatters: This is not a one-off event. Three national all-time records falling in two days, with hundreds of deaths and critical infrastructure failing, signals that Europe's buildings, power grids, and health systems were built for a different climate. June heat is supposed to be a prelude; the peak of summer is still ahead.

sections.heading: Venezuela's Twin Earthquakes: 1,400+ Dead, 50,000 Missing
sections.body:
Two earthquakes — magnitudes 7.2 and 7.5, seconds apart on Wednesday — struck Venezuela, killing at least 1,430 people and injuring more than 4,300. The UN estimates around 50,000 people remain missing. Hundreds of buildings collapsed across the coastal state of La Guaira and the Caracas area, where cement shortages caused by the collapse of the state-owned cement industry after nationalization under Chávez left housing stock dangerously vulnerable.

Interim President Delcy Rodríguez declared a state of emergency and accepted offers of help from the Trump administration and right-wing governments in Chile, the Dominican Republic, and El Salvador — a notable break from Maduro's policies. Rodríguez also replaced generals running the housing and electricity ministries with civilian specialists, implicitly acknowledging that two decades of military appointments degraded state capacity. But on the ground, residents described a response that is inadequate to the scale of destruction. 'No one from the government has come here,' said Glendys Delgado, a resident near two collapsed buildings in Caracas. Rescue teams from Mexico, Spain, the US, and the UK have arrived, but there is an acute shortage of heavy equipment. Some people can still be heard groaning under debris that volunteers cannot move without machinery.
sections.whyItMatters: A 7.5-magnitude earthquake hitting a country with collapsed infrastructure is a catastrophe multiplier. The death toll will likely rise far higher as the rescue window closes. Rodríguez's willingness to accept US aid is a genuine break from the Maduro era, but the response is being led by the same military establishment whose mismanagement contributed to the vulnerability.

sections.heading: Ukraine Escalates Long-Range Strikes, Europe Aligns for Ankara Summit
sections.body:
Ukraine significantly escalated its long-range strike campaign this week. On Monday, Ukrainian forces launched a coordinated strike on Russian logistics across the Kerch Strait, setting ablaze the Port of Kavkaz oil transshipment complex and the TES-Terminal-1 oil depot in occupied Kerch. At least three ferries burned at the Kerch Strait crossing, and four S-400 radar stations and two Pantsir air defense systems were disabled on the bridge. Crimea's occupation governor ordered all gas stations to stop selling fuel to non-state entities and barred fuel sales in Sevastopol entirely.

On Saturday, Ukraine struck deeper, hitting the Titan-Barrikady facility in Volgograd — which manufactures launchers for the Iskander-M missile system — using FP-5 Flamingo missiles. Volgograd's governor confirmed 10 people were wounded and production facilities were damaged. President Zelenskyy said he had ordered a 40-day influence operation aimed at 'compelling Russia to end the war' after US peace efforts yielded no breakthrough.

In Berlin, the E5 group — Germany, Britain, France, Italy, and Poland — pledged to 'further substantially support Ukraine' including through sanctions and energy-sector resilience. All five leaders will attend the NATO summit in Ankara on July 7-8, where secretary general Mark Rutte will push members to turn last year's 5% GDP defence spending pledge into concrete capability commitments. The gap between pledge and reality remains wide: Spain, Belgium, and Canada have only recently announced plans to reach 2%.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine is demonstrating it can still impose significant costs on Russia — not on the battlefield, but on logistics and military-industrial production. The 40-day escalation order and the Volgograd strike signal Kyiv's calculation that pressure, not diplomacy, is the path to negotiations. The Ankara summit will test whether NATO's 5% pledge is real or becomes the next 2% — a benchmark most members treat as optional.

bottomLine: The week that began with a 60-day roadmap from Switzerland ended with American bombs on Iranian soil, Iranian missiles on American bases in Kuwait and Bahrain, and a ceasefire in genuine danger of collapse. Two escalation cycles in three days have revealed the MOU's core flaw: both sides claim the right to enforce their interpretation through force. Meanwhile, Israel and Lebanon signed a peace framework that Hezbollah immediately rejected and Israeli strikes continued within hours. In Washington, Trump's $87.6 billion war funding request is meeting the most significant congressional rebellion of his presidency. Europe suffered through its worst heatwave in recorded history — three national all-time records in two days, hundreds dead, nuclear plants offline. Venezuela's earthquakes killed over 1,400 with tens of thousands still missing. Ukraine demonstrated it can reach deep into Russia, hitting a missile plant in Volgograd. Across every theater this week — the Gulf, Lebanon, Washington, Europe, Caracas — the same pattern held: the systems designed to manage crisis are being tested past their limits, and several are breaking.
sources: AP, BBC, Reuters, Al Jazeera, ISW, Wall Street Journal, CBS News, Axios, Clarksons, Lloyd's List Intelligence, Windward, Kpler, Argus Media, World Weather Attribution, Copernicus, IMO, Centcom, US Treasury, IAEA, New York Times
